candidate_problems.text: What are the minimal and sufficient components of an 'error-correcting social structure' for stable cooperation, and how do these components trade off against one another across environments? The conjecture names detection, punishment, memory, routing around defectors, and revisability, but leaves unresolved which of these are load-bearing, whether some can substitute for others, and where thresholds or phase transitions lie.

How can error-correcting cooperation avoid collapsing into false accusation, reputational lock-in, surveillance overreach, or cartelized punishment? The conjecture treats criticism, memory, and selection as cooperation-supporting, but an open question is how such mechanisms distinguish real defection from noise, error, strategic slander, and changing norms without creating self-reinforcing epistemic injustice.

By what dynamics do decentralized error-correcting institutions emerge, improve, and remain adaptable under adversarial pressure rather than requiring prior trust or central design? The conjecture explains persistence better than origin: it is unresolved how groups bootstrap reliable gossip, auditing, sanction coordination, and norm revision when those very capacities seem to presuppose some existing cooperative order.
